description: Foundational Data Infrastructure and Standards. This theme covers the technical specifications, terminologies, and core data definitions that form the common language for all health information exchange. It includes the adoption and enhancement of standards like FHIR for APIs, USCDI for data elements, and various terminologies (SNOMED, LOINC) for semantic meaning, as well as standards for data quality and provenance
detailed_guidelines: This theme covers the technical specifications, terminologies, and core data definitions that form the common language for all health information exchange. It includes the adoption and enhancement of standards like FHIR for APIs, USCDI for data elements, and various terminologies (SNOMED, LOINC) for semantic meaning, as well as standards for data quality and provenance. This theme does not cover the national networks (like TEFCA) that use these standards, nor the specific applications (like AI) that consume the data. The defining characteristic is its focus on the "dictionary and grammar" of health data. This matters because without a universally understood and consistently implemented set of standards, true interoperability is impossible.
detailedDescription: This theme covers the technical specifications, terminologies, and core data definitions that form the common language for all health information exchange. It includes the adoption and enhancement of standards like FHIR for APIs, USCDI for data elements, and various terminologies (SNOMED, LOINC) for semantic meaning, as well as standards for data quality and provenance. This theme does not cover the national networks (like TEFCA) that use these standards, nor the specific applications (like AI) that consume the data. The defining characteristic is its focus on the "dictionary and grammar" of health data. This matters because without a universally understood and consistently implemented set of standards, true interoperability is impossible.

description: Mandating and Expanding FHIR API Adoption
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme captures the widespread recommendation to mandate and expand the use of FHIR as the primary standard for all health data exchange. It includes calls for payers, providers, and vendors to adopt FHIR for patient access, provider-to-provider exchange, prior authorization, and administrative workflows, as well as proposals to phase out older document-based methods. This is more specific than general standards advocacy, focusing solely on FHIR as the cornerstone technology. This matters because a vast majority of tech-focused commenters see universal FHIR adoption as the most critical step toward a modern, interoperable health ecosystem.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme captures the widespread recommendation to mandate and expand the use of FHIR as the primary standard for all health data exchange. It includes calls for payers, providers, and vendors to adopt FHIR for patient access, provider-to-provider exchange, prior authorization, and administrative workflows, as well as proposals to phase out older document-based methods. This is more specific than general standards advocacy, focusing solely on FHIR as the cornerstone technology. This matters because a vast majority of tech-focused commenters see universal FHIR adoption as the most critical step toward a modern, interoperable health ecosystem.

description: Improving FHIR API Functionality and Performance
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme details recommendations to improve how FHIR APIs are implemented and what they can do in the real world. It includes calls to ensure APIs are bi-directional (read/write), support granular consent, and include specific parameters like `_since` for incremental data requests. It also covers the need for strict API performance and reliability standards, voluntary conformance testing programs, and a "Neighborhood Watch" to report non-performing APIs. This is distinct from simply mandating FHIR, as it focuses on the quality, consistency, and reliability of the implementation. This is critical because varied, poor-quality, and unreliable implementations undermine the value of the standard.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme details recommendations to improve how FHIR APIs are implemented and what they can do in the real world. It includes calls to ensure APIs are bi-directional (read/write), support granular consent, and include specific parameters like `_since` for incremental data requests. It also covers the need for strict API performance and reliability standards, voluntary conformance testing programs, and a "Neighborhood Watch" to report non-performing APIs. This is distinct from simply mandating FHIR, as it focuses on the quality, consistency, and reliability of the implementation. This is critical because varied, poor-quality, and unreliable implementations undermine the value of the standard.

description: Enabling Actionable Workflows with Advanced APIs
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme covers recommendations to expand API capabilities beyond simple data retrieval to support actionable, event-driven workflows. It includes specific requests for mandating support for FHIR Subscriptions for real-time notifications, CDS Hooks for clinical decision support integration, and full CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) capabilities. This is different from basic data access, as it focuses on making data interactive, useful for performing tasks, and enabling proactive care. This is crucial for building next-generation applications that streamline workflows and enable timely interventions.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme covers recommendations to expand API capabilities beyond simple data retrieval to support actionable, event-driven workflows. It includes specific requests for mandating support for FHIR Subscriptions for real-time notifications, CDS Hooks for clinical decision support integration, and full CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) capabilities. This is different from basic data access, as it focuses on making data interactive, useful for performing tasks, and enabling proactive care. This is crucial for building next-generation applications that streamline workflows and enable timely interventions.

description: Expanding and Accelerating USCDI Development
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme focuses on recommendations to improve the U.S. Core Data for Interoperability (USCDI) standard. This includes calls to expand the USCDI to include more data classes and elements, such as clinical notes, imaging, pharmacy data, SDOH, behavioral and maternal health, and patient-generated health data (PGHD). It also includes critiques that the standard is not comprehensive enough and calls to accelerate the review and approval process for new versions. This is distinct from other terminology standards by focusing solely on the ONC-managed USCDI. This matters because USCDI is a core requirement for certified health IT, and its limitations directly impact what data is exchanged in a standardized way.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme focuses on recommendations to improve the U.S. Core Data for Interoperability (USCDI) standard. This includes calls to expand the USCDI to include more data classes and elements, such as clinical notes, imaging, pharmacy data, SDOH, behavioral and maternal health, and patient-generated health data (PGHD). It also includes critiques that the standard is not comprehensive enough and calls to accelerate the review and approval process for new versions. This is distinct from other terminology standards by focusing solely on the ONC-managed USCDI. This matters because USCDI is a core requirement for certified health IT, and its limitations directly impact what data is exchanged in a standardized way.

description: Harmonizing Terminology and Semantic Standards. This sub-theme addresses the need for standardized clinical and administrative vocabularies to ensure data has a consistent meaning. It includes recommendations to adopt or expand specific code sets like SNOMED CT, LOINC, and RxNorm; to map other systems like HCPCS to these standards; and to establish a national terminology server. It also covers calls for formal ontologies, cross-agency terminology harmonization (e.g., for 'digital health'), and adoption of standards like openEHR
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme addresses the need for standardized clinical and administrative vocabularies to ensure data has a consistent meaning. It includes recommendations to adopt or expand specific code sets like SNOMED CT, LOINC, and RxNorm; to map other systems like HCPCS to these standards; and to establish a national terminology server. It also covers calls for formal ontologies, cross-agency terminology harmonization (e.g., for 'digital health'), and adoption of standards like openEHR. This theme is about the *meaning* of data, not just its structure (FHIR). This is critical for data analysts and AI developers who need consistent, unambiguous data to perform their work accurately.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme addresses the need for standardized clinical and administrative vocabularies to ensure data has a consistent meaning. It includes recommendations to adopt or expand specific code sets like SNOMED CT, LOINC, and RxNorm; to map other systems like HCPCS to these standards; and to establish a national terminology server. It also covers calls for formal ontologies, cross-agency terminology harmonization (e.g., for 'digital health'), and adoption of standards like openEHR. This theme is about the *meaning* of data, not just its structure (FHIR). This is critical for data analysts and AI developers who need consistent, unambiguous data to perform their work accurately.

description: Ensuring Data Quality, Provenance, and Integrity
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme captures concerns about the accuracy, validity, and trustworthiness of electronic health data. It includes recommendations to institute data quality requirements, develop data quality rating frameworks, mandate standardized data provenance labeling (especially for AI-generated data), and address the high frequency of errors from human input, misdiagnosis, and fraud. This is distinct from data standards, focusing on the correctness and origin of the data itself. This matters because exchanging poor-quality or untrustworthy data is dangerous and undermines all interoperability efforts.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme captures concerns about the accuracy, validity, and trustworthiness of electronic health data. It includes recommendations to institute data quality requirements, develop data quality rating frameworks, mandate standardized data provenance labeling (especially for AI-generated data), and address the high frequency of errors from human input, misdiagnosis, and fraud. This is distinct from data standards, focusing on the correctness and origin of the data itself. This matters because exchanging poor-quality or untrustworthy data is dangerous and undermines all interoperability efforts.

description: Integrating Specialized and Unstructured Data
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme addresses the challenge of making siloed, non-standard, or unstructured data interoperable. It includes specific calls to integrate medical imaging (DICOM) and oral health (dental) data into national frameworks, and to require certified health IT to transform unstructured data (notes, PDFs, faxes) into structured, computable formats accessible via API. This is distinct from USCDI expansion, as it focuses on historically separate domains and formats. This matters because vast amounts of critical clinical information reside in these isolated siloes.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme addresses the challenge of making siloed, non-standard, or unstructured data interoperable. It includes specific calls to integrate medical imaging (DICOM) and oral health (dental) data into national frameworks, and to require certified health IT to transform unstructured data (notes, PDFs, faxes) into structured, computable formats accessible via API. This is distinct from USCDI expansion, as it focuses on historically separate domains and formats. This matters because vast amounts of critical clinical information reside in these isolated siloes.

description: Regulatory Frameworks and Governance. This theme encompasses comments about the role of federal agencies in shaping the health technology ecosystem through regulation, certification, and enforcement. It includes recommendations for how CMS and ONC should approach certification, regulation, and innovation, as well as critiques of current approaches like information blocking enforcement and price transparency
detailed_guidelines: This theme encompasses comments about the role of federal agencies in shaping the health technology ecosystem through regulation, certification, and enforcement. It includes recommendations for how CMS and ONC should approach certification, regulation, and innovation, as well as critiques of current approaches like information blocking enforcement and price transparency. This theme is about the high-level strategy and function of the regulators themselves.
detailedDescription: This theme encompasses comments about the role of federal agencies in shaping the health technology ecosystem through regulation, certification, and enforcement. It includes recommendations for how CMS and ONC should approach certification, regulation, and innovation, as well as critiques of current approaches like information blocking enforcement and price transparency. This theme is about the high-level strategy and function of the regulators themselves.

description: Strengthening Information Blocking Enforcement and Rules
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme focuses on recommendations to more effectively identify, penalize, and prevent the practice of information blocking. It includes calls for stronger and more transparent enforcement with significant penalties, clearer definitions of what constitutes blocking, and revisions to narrow the exceptions (e.g., Fees, Manner, Infeasibility) that commenters fear are being abused as loopholes. It also includes critiques of the ineffective complaint reporting process. This is crucial because commenters believe information blocking by dominant market players is a primary barrier to interoperability.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme focuses on recommendations to more effectively identify, penalize, and prevent the practice of information blocking. It includes calls for stronger and more transparent enforcement with significant penalties, clearer definitions of what constitutes blocking, and revisions to narrow the exceptions (e.g., Fees, Manner, Infeasibility) that commenters fear are being abused as loopholes. It also includes critiques of the ineffective complaint reporting process. This is crucial because commenters believe information blocking by dominant market players is a primary barrier to interoperability.

description: Reforming Health IT Certification
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme focuses on feedback for the ONC Health IT Certification Program. It includes recommendations to shift from a checklist-based model to one that is performance-based, prioritize API-enabled capabilities over specific software functions, create alternative or modular certification tracks (e.g., for VBC or specific innovations), and make the process less burdensome and costly. The focus is on making certification more meaningful. This is critical because certification dictates the features and capabilities of health IT products on the market.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme focuses on feedback for the ONC Health IT Certification Program. It includes recommendations to shift from a checklist-based model to one that is performance-based, prioritize API-enabled capabilities over specific software functions, create alternative or modular certification tracks (e.g., for VBC or specific innovations), and make the process less burdensome and costly. The focus is on making certification more meaningful. This is critical because certification dictates the features and capabilities of health IT products on the market.

description: Improving Price and Benefit Transparency
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme encompasses comments related to making the cost of healthcare services and plan benefits more transparent to consumers. It includes concerns that existing price transparency files are inaccurate or unusable, and recommendations to mandate this data be available via standardized APIs for both FFS and Medicare Advantage plans, and to strengthen enforcement for non-compliance. The key feature is its focus on making cost and benefit data accessible and computable. This matters because effective transparency is seen as key to empowering consumers.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme encompasses comments related to making the cost of healthcare services and plan benefits more transparent to consumers. It includes concerns that existing price transparency files are inaccurate or unusable, and recommendations to mandate this data be available via standardized APIs for both FFS and Medicare Advantage plans, and to strengthen enforcement for non-compliance. The key feature is its focus on making cost and benefit data accessible and computable. This matters because effective transparency is seen as key to empowering consumers.

description: Streamlining Cross-Agency Data Exchange
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme addresses the need for better data sharing and coordination between different federal and state government agencies. The primary example is the need to streamline the flow of health information to the Social Security Administration (SSA) for disability determinations, which is currently a slow, burdensome manual process. It also includes concerns about state agencies failing to comply with federal tech standards. This matters because citizens often interact with multiple agencies, and a lack of data sharing creates massive inefficiency.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme addresses the need for better data sharing and coordination between different federal and state government agencies. The primary example is the need to streamline the flow of health information to the Social Security Administration (SSA) for disability determinations, which is currently a slow, burdensome manual process. It also includes concerns about state agencies failing to comply with federal tech standards. This matters because citizens often interact with multiple agencies, and a lack of data sharing creates massive inefficiency.

description: Improving the Rulemaking and Standards Development Process
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme captures meta-level comments about how CMS and ONC should create policy. It includes strong calls for direct, resourced engagement with end-users (clinicians, HIM professionals) throughout the development process and for robust, "true" real-world testing of standards and policies before they are mandated. The goal is to make policy development more grounded and practical. This matters because a disconnect between policymakers and front-line users is seen as a primary reason for burdensome and ineffective regulations.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme captures meta-level comments about how CMS and ONC should create policy. It includes strong calls for direct, resourced engagement with end-users (clinicians, HIM professionals) throughout the development process and for robust, "true" real-world testing of standards and policies before they are mandated. The goal is to make policy development more grounded and practical. This matters because a disconnect between policymakers and front-line users is seen as a primary reason for burdensome and ineffective regulations.

description: Systemic Critiques and Alternative Visions
detailed_guidelines: This theme captures broad, fundamental critiques of the current healthcare IT paradigm and proposals for radical redesigns of the entire system. This includes comments that question the premise of current initiatives like VBC or offer comprehensive, alternative architectures for the national health data ecosystem. These are distinct from incremental reforms, advocating instead for a complete overhaul. This matters because it reflects deep dissatisfaction with the current approach and offers competing paradigms for the future.
detailedDescription: This theme captures broad, fundamental critiques of the current healthcare IT paradigm and proposals for radical redesigns of the entire system. This includes comments that question the premise of current initiatives like VBC or offer comprehensive, alternative architectures for the national health data ecosystem. These are distinct from incremental reforms, advocating instead for a complete overhaul. This matters because it reflects deep dissatisfaction with the current approach and offers competing paradigms for the future.

description: Advocacy for a Centralized National Health Data System
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme captures proposals for creating a single, central, government-led data repository or EMR system to house all patient health information. It includes specific recommendations for a National Health Data Repository (NHDR), a national data exchange hub, or a mandate for one universal outpatient and one universal inpatient EMR system. This is distinct from federated models like TEFCA. Proponents see this as the only way to truly overcome fragmentation, data siloing, and information loss.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme captures proposals for creating a single, central, government-led data repository or EMR system to house all patient health information. It includes specific recommendations for a National Health Data Repository (NHDR), a national data exchange hub, or a mandate for one universal outpatient and one universal inpatient EMR system. This is distinct from federated models like TEFCA. Proponents see this as the only way to truly overcome fragmentation, data siloing, and information loss.

description: Advocacy for Patient-Controlled Health Data Architectures
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme captures proposals to redesign the national health data architecture around patient-owned and controlled data repositories. It includes specific visions like "Health Data Banks" or "Cures Act Repositories," where the patient's record is the central source of truth and they grant access to providers and apps. This is a comprehensive, alternative architectural vision that contrasts with both provider-centric and centralized-government models. This matters because it presents a fully-formed, patient-centric paradigm for achieving interoperability.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme captures proposals to redesign the national health data architecture around patient-owned and controlled data repositories. It includes specific visions like "Health Data Banks" or "Cures Act Repositories," where the patient's record is the central source of truth and they grant access to providers and apps. This is a comprehensive, alternative architectural vision that contrasts with both provider-centric and centralized-government models. This matters because it presents a fully-formed, patient-centric paradigm for achieving interoperability.

description: Advocacy for Market-Driven vs. Government-Mandated Innovation
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme presents the argument that government intervention, particularly mandating specific standards like TEFCA and FHIR, stifles innovation. The commenters advocate for letting market forces and pay-for-performance incentives drive the evolution of health IT infrastructure, arguing that modern, cloud-based networks will emerge naturally and more effectively than government-imposed legacy standards. This is a core philosophical disagreement with the premise of most other comments. It is a significant counterpoint to the widespread calls for more regulation.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme presents the argument that government intervention, particularly mandating specific standards like TEFCA and FHIR, stifles innovation. The commenters advocate for letting market forces and pay-for-performance incentives drive the evolution of health IT infrastructure, arguing that modern, cloud-based networks will emerge naturally and more effectively than government-imposed legacy standards. This is a core philosophical disagreement with the premise of most other comments. It is a significant counterpoint to the widespread calls for more regulation.

description: Fundamental Distrust of the Digital Health Enterprise
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme captures a deep-seated skepticism and wholesale rejection of the digital health paradigm. It includes beliefs that digitization has made providers negligent, that the risks of data breaches outweigh any benefits, that the push for technology is a form of "genocide" against the elderly, and that traditional, non-digital Medicare is superior. This is distinct from specific privacy concerns, representing a fundamental opposition to the digital transformation of healthcare. This is important as it reflects a user perspective completely at odds with policy goals.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme captures a deep-seated skepticism and wholesale rejection of the digital health paradigm. It includes beliefs that digitization has made providers negligent, that the risks of data breaches outweigh any benefits, that the push for technology is a form of "genocide" against the elderly, and that traditional, non-digital Medicare is superior. This is distinct from specific privacy concerns, representing a fundamental opposition to the digital transformation of healthcare. This is important as it reflects a user perspective completely at odds with policy goals.

description: Advocacy for Advanced Technical Architectures
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme covers proposals to rebuild the health technology ecosystem on newer, more advanced technical foundations. It includes specific proposals for a real-time, event-driven data streaming architecture (using technologies like Apache Kafka), a federally organized system based on a patient-controlled blockchain-enabled PHR, and an infrastructure built on "agentic AI" and offline-first principles. These are specific technical architecture proposals that differ from the standard API-query model. This matters as they offer different foundational approaches to data infrastructure modernization.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme covers proposals to rebuild the health technology ecosystem on newer, more advanced technical foundations. It includes specific proposals for a real-time, event-driven data streaming architecture (using technologies like Apache Kafka), a federally organized system based on a patient-controlled blockchain-enabled PHR, and an infrastructure built on "agentic AI" and offline-first principles. These are specific technical architecture proposals that differ from the standard API-query model. This matters as they offer different foundational approaches to data infrastructure modernization.

description: National Data Exchange Frameworks and Networks. This theme addresses the high-level networks, directories, and agreements designed to facilitate nationwide health information exchange. It includes comments on national frameworks like TEFCA, the role of regional Health Information Exchanges (HIEs), and the need for essential public infrastructure like a national provider directory
detailed_guidelines: This theme addresses the high-level networks, directories, and agreements designed to facilitate nationwide health information exchange. It includes comments on national frameworks like TEFCA, the role of regional Health Information Exchanges (HIEs), and the need for essential public infrastructure like a national provider directory. This theme focuses on the "highways" of data exchange, separating it from the "rules of the road" (standards) or the "vehicles" (applications). This matters because these frameworks are seen as the primary vehicle for achieving scalable, nationwide interoperability beyond point-to-point connections.
detailedDescription: This theme addresses the high-level networks, directories, and agreements designed to facilitate nationwide health information exchange. It includes comments on national frameworks like TEFCA, the role of regional Health Information Exchanges (HIEs), and the need for essential public infrastructure like a national provider directory. This theme focuses on the "highways" of data exchange, separating it from the "rules of the road" (standards) or the "vehicles" (applications). This matters because these frameworks are seen as the primary vehicle for achieving scalable, nationwide interoperability beyond point-to-point connections.

description: Improving the Trusted Exchange Framework and Common Agreement (TEFCA)
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme captures all feedback on TEFCA, including critiques and recommendations for its improvement. It includes calls to mandate TEFCA participation for payers and labs, expand Exchange Purposes to include payment and public benefits, accelerate the transition to FHIR-based exchange, and ensure it supports individual access and complements existing HIEs. It also includes critiques that TEFCA is too limited, overly complex, and not designed for population-level use cases. This theme is separate from other infrastructure elements. This matters because TEFCA is the federal government's primary strategy for nationwide interoperability, and its success is a major concern.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme captures all feedback on TEFCA, including critiques and recommendations for its improvement. It includes calls to mandate TEFCA participation for payers and labs, expand Exchange Purposes to include payment and public benefits, accelerate the transition to FHIR-based exchange, and ensure it supports individual access and complements existing HIEs. It also includes critiques that TEFCA is too limited, overly complex, and not designed for population-level use cases. This theme is separate from other infrastructure elements. This matters because TEFCA is the federal government's primary strategy for nationwide interoperability, and its success is a major concern.

description: Establishing a Nationwide Provider and Endpoint Directory
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme covers the numerous recommendations to create a centralized, accurate, and publicly accessible national directory of provider FHIR endpoints. It includes suggestions on governance (CMS, neutral entity), funding (QHP fees), and data content (NPI-level granularity, endpoint metadata, supported capabilities, scheduling availability). It also calls for consolidating duplicative directory requirements to reduce provider burden. This is a specific infrastructure proposal aimed at solving the "phone book" problem for healthcare APIs. This is critical because without a reliable directory, automated, scalable data exchange is impossible.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme covers the numerous recommendations to create a centralized, accurate, and publicly accessible national directory of provider FHIR endpoints. It includes suggestions on governance (CMS, neutral entity), funding (QHP fees), and data content (NPI-level granularity, endpoint metadata, supported capabilities, scheduling availability). It also calls for consolidating duplicative directory requirements to reduce provider burden. This is a specific infrastructure proposal aimed at solving the "phone book" problem for healthcare APIs. This is critical because without a reliable directory, automated, scalable data exchange is impossible.

description: Defining the Role of Health Information Exchanges (HIEs)
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme addresses the role of state and regional Health Information Exchanges (HIEs) in the national ecosystem. It includes recommendations to leverage HIEs as drivers and "incubators" for FHIR adoption, to mandate they provide patient access portals and FHIR APIs, and to ensure they are well-integrated with national frameworks like TEFCA. This theme is distinct from TEFCA, focusing on the specific role of these existing regional entities. This matters because HIEs are a vital part of the interoperability landscape that can support providers, especially smaller ones, in meeting federal mandates.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme addresses the role of state and regional Health Information Exchanges (HIEs) in the national ecosystem. It includes recommendations to leverage HIEs as drivers and "incubators" for FHIR adoption, to mandate they provide patient access portals and FHIR APIs, and to ensure they are well-integrated with national frameworks like TEFCA. This theme is distinct from TEFCA, focusing on the specific role of these existing regional entities. This matters because HIEs are a vital part of the interoperability landscape that can support providers, especially smaller ones, in meeting federal mandates.

description: Standardizing Patient Matching and Identity Resolution
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme focuses on the critical and unresolved challenge of accurately matching patient records across different systems. It captures the intense debate between two main solutions: strong advocacy for repealing the congressional ban and establishing a national Unique Patient Identifier (UPI), versus support for federated digital identity credentials. It also includes concerns about the poor quality of demographic data that fuels matching errors. This is a foundational problem for data quality and patient safety. This matters because patient matching errors lead to fragmented records, duplicate testing, and severe patient safety incidents.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme focuses on the critical and unresolved challenge of accurately matching patient records across different systems. It captures the intense debate between two main solutions: strong advocacy for repealing the congressional ban and establishing a national Unique Patient Identifier (UPI), versus support for federated digital identity credentials. It also includes concerns about the poor quality of demographic data that fuels matching errors. This is a foundational problem for data quality and patient safety. This matters because patient matching errors lead to fragmented records, duplicate testing, and severe patient safety incidents.

description: Digital Health Technology, AI, and Innovation. This theme covers the ecosystem of specific digital health tools, platforms, and applications, including their development, adoption, regulation, and reimbursement. This includes telehealth, remote patient monitoring (RPM), digital therapeutics (DTx), patient-facing apps, and artificial intelligence
detailed_guidelines: This theme covers the ecosystem of specific digital health tools, platforms, and applications, including their development, adoption, regulation, and reimbursement. This includes telehealth, remote patient monitoring (RPM), digital therapeutics (DTx), patient-facing apps, and artificial intelligence. This theme is distinct from foundational interoperability by focusing on the applications that *use* the data, rather than the pipes that move it. This matters because these technologies represent the "front end" of digital transformation and are seen as key drivers for improving care and efficiency.
detailedDescription: This theme covers the ecosystem of specific digital health tools, platforms, and applications, including their development, adoption, regulation, and reimbursement. This includes telehealth, remote patient monitoring (RPM), digital therapeutics (DTx), patient-facing apps, and artificial intelligence. This theme is distinct from foundational interoperability by focusing on the applications that *use* the data, rather than the pipes that move it. This matters because these technologies represent the "front end" of digital transformation and are seen as key drivers for improving care and efficiency.

description: Making Telehealth Flexibilities Permanent
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme specifically captures the widespread call to make temporary, pandemic-related Medicare telehealth flexibilities permanent. This includes removing geographic and originating site restrictions, continuing coverage for audio-only services, waiving in-person requirements for telemental health, and making virtual direct supervision permanent. It also includes counter-arguments about potential for misdiagnosis and lower quality of care. This is a major advocacy point for providers and patients who argue that letting these flexibilities expire would severely restrict access to care.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme specifically captures the widespread call to make temporary, pandemic-related Medicare telehealth flexibilities permanent. This includes removing geographic and originating site restrictions, continuing coverage for audio-only services, waiving in-person requirements for telemental health, and making virtual direct supervision permanent. It also includes counter-arguments about potential for misdiagnosis and lower quality of care. This is a major advocacy point for providers and patients who argue that letting these flexibilities expire would severely restrict access to care.

description: Reforming Remote Monitoring (RPM/RTM) Reimbursement and Rules
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme captures recommendations to fix Medicare's payment policies and rules for Remote Patient/Therapeutic Monitoring. It includes proposals to increase reimbursement rates, simplify or eliminate restrictive billing requirements (e.g., the 16-days-per-month rule), reduce patient cost-sharing, and expand coverage to more conditions and use cases like medication adherence. This is a specific set of reforms targeting a service category that commenters find overly rigid and misaligned with clinical needs. This matters because RPM is seen as essential for proactive disease management, but adoption is limited by cost and complexity.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme captures recommendations to fix Medicare's payment policies and rules for Remote Patient/Therapeutic Monitoring. It includes proposals to increase reimbursement rates, simplify or eliminate restrictive billing requirements (e.g., the 16-days-per-month rule), reduce patient cost-sharing, and expand coverage to more conditions and use cases like medication adherence. This is a specific set of reforms targeting a service category that commenters find overly rigid and misaligned with clinical needs. This matters because RPM is seen as essential for proactive disease management, but adoption is limited by cost and complexity.

description: Establishing Reimbursement Pathways for Digital Therapeutics (DTx)
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme encompasses comments on the need for clear and adequate reimbursement for digital therapeutics, a class of software-based medical interventions. It includes strong criticism of the current approach of deferring payment to MACs, which has led to inconsistent and inadequate rates, and calls for CMS to establish a national price or price floor for these products. It also includes proposals to integrate DTx into VBC models and treatment pathways. This matters because developers argue that without predictable and sufficient reimbursement, the market for these innovative therapies cannot be sustained.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme encompasses comments on the need for clear and adequate reimbursement for digital therapeutics, a class of software-based medical interventions. It includes strong criticism of the current approach of deferring payment to MACs, which has led to inconsistent and inadequate rates, and calls for CMS to establish a national price or price floor for these products. It also includes proposals to integrate DTx into VBC models and treatment pathways. This matters because developers argue that without predictable and sufficient reimbursement, the market for these innovative therapies cannot be sustained.

description: Fostering a Healthy Digital Health App Ecosystem
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme concerns the market and regulatory environment for third-party digital health applications. It includes recommendations for CMS to stimulate developer interest by providing secure sandboxes with realistic test data, creating "CMS Trusted App" certification or marketplaces, and establishing sustainable business models that don't rely on selling user data. This is about fostering a healthy and innovative "app store" for healthcare. This matters because a vibrant developer ecosystem is needed to create the tools that will leverage interoperable data.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme concerns the market and regulatory environment for third-party digital health applications. It includes recommendations for CMS to stimulate developer interest by providing secure sandboxes with realistic test data, creating "CMS Trusted App" certification or marketplaces, and establishing sustainable business models that don't rely on selling user data. This is about fostering a healthy and innovative "app store" for healthcare. This matters because a vibrant developer ecosystem is needed to create the tools that will leverage interoperable data.

description: Integrating Tools with EHRs and Clinical Workflows
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme addresses the critical challenge of integrating new digital health tools with existing Electronic Health Record (EHR) systems and provider workflows. It includes concerns that standalone tools create data silos and increase provider burden, with recommendations to mandate modular, "plug-and-play" EHR integration via APIs like SMART on FHIR. The focus is on ensuring new technologies fit seamlessly into practice. This matters because poor integration and workflow disruption are key reasons for low adoption of innovative technologies.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme addresses the critical challenge of integrating new digital health tools with existing Electronic Health Record (EHR) systems and provider workflows. It includes concerns that standalone tools create data silos and increase provider burden, with recommendations to mandate modular, "plug-and-play" EHR integration via APIs like SMART on FHIR. The focus is on ensuring new technologies fit seamlessly into practice. This matters because poor integration and workflow disruption are key reasons for low adoption of innovative technologies.

description: Stimulating Innovation via Pilots and Sandboxes
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme gathers recommendations for CMS and other agencies to take a more active role in fostering innovation. It includes numerous calls to establish "sandboxes" for developers to test applications, fund pilot programs for new technologies, create "Innovator Fast Lanes" to speed up development, and convene prize challenges. The theme is defined by its focus on creating safe, structured environments for experimentation. This matters to innovators who need access to data and realistic testing environments to build and validate their products.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme gathers recommendations for CMS and other agencies to take a more active role in fostering innovation. It includes numerous calls to establish "sandboxes" for developers to test applications, fund pilot programs for new technologies, create "Innovator Fast Lanes" to speed up development, and convene prize challenges. The theme is defined by its focus on creating safe, structured environments for experimentation. This matters to innovators who need access to data and realistic testing environments to build and validate their products.

description: Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Healthcare. This theme is dedicated to the opportunities and challenges of using Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in the health ecosystem, which was a topic of major focus. It includes specific AI use cases for clinical and administrative tasks, as well as significant concerns about governance, safety, transparency, bias, and liability
detailed_guidelines: This theme is dedicated to the opportunities and challenges of using Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in the health ecosystem, which was a topic of major focus. It includes specific AI use cases for clinical and administrative tasks, as well as significant concerns about governance, safety, transparency, bias, and liability. This theme is separated from other digital tools due to the transformative potential and unique risks associated with AI. This matters because AI is seen as a revolutionary force that requires careful and specific policy guidance to be deployed safely and ethically.
detailedDescription: This theme is dedicated to the opportunities and challenges of using Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in the health ecosystem, which was a topic of major focus. It includes specific AI use cases for clinical and administrative tasks, as well as significant concerns about governance, safety, transparency, bias, and liability. This theme is separated from other digital tools due to the transformative potential and unique risks associated with AI. This matters because AI is seen as a revolutionary force that requires careful and specific policy guidance to be deployed safely and ethically.

description: AI for Clinical and Administrative Decision Support
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme covers AI applications designed to assist providers and administrators in their professional duties. It includes proposals for AI-powered clinical decision support based on coverage rules, AI-driven prior authorization automation, platforms that surface care gaps or risk-stratify patients, and tools that extract structured data from unstructured notes. This is distinct from patient-facing AI, focusing on professional use cases. This matters because commenters see AI as a key to reducing administrative burden and improving the quality and consistency of clinical decisions.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme covers AI applications designed to assist providers and administrators in their professional duties. It includes proposals for AI-powered clinical decision support based on coverage rules, AI-driven prior authorization automation, platforms that surface care gaps or risk-stratify patients, and tools that extract structured data from unstructured notes. This is distinct from patient-facing AI, focusing on professional use cases. This matters because commenters see AI as a key to reducing administrative burden and improving the quality and consistency of clinical decisions.

description: AI for Patient and Caregiver Engagement
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme focuses on AI-powered tools designed for direct interaction with patients, caregivers, and beneficiaries. It includes proposals for conversational AI assistants (e.g., Clover's Counterpart), interactive AI avatars for remote monitoring, AI-powered Medicare coverage assistants, and "Digital Twin" companions to guide patients. This is distinct from clinical/admin AI by its focus on the patient or caregiver as the end user. This is important because these tools are proposed as a way to scale patient support, improve health literacy, and enhance navigation of the healthcare system.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme focuses on AI-powered tools designed for direct interaction with patients, caregivers, and beneficiaries. It includes proposals for conversational AI assistants (e.g., Clover's Counterpart), interactive AI avatars for remote monitoring, AI-powered Medicare coverage assistants, and "Digital Twin" companions to guide patients. This is distinct from clinical/admin AI by its focus on the patient or caregiver as the end user. This is important because these tools are proposed as a way to scale patient support, improve health literacy, and enhance navigation of the healthcare system.

description: AI Governance, Safety, and Validation
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme captures concerns and recommendations related to ensuring AI tools are safe, effective, transparent, and equitable. It includes calls for CMS-led validation programs, voluntary "Clinical AI Safety Label" certifications, regulatory "sandboxes," and requirements for algorithmic bias testing and explainable AI (XAI). The focus is on establishing rules and guardrails for AI development and deployment. This matters because a lack of trust in the safety and reliability of AI is a major barrier to its adoption.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme captures concerns and recommendations related to ensuring AI tools are safe, effective, transparent, and equitable. It includes calls for CMS-led validation programs, voluntary "Clinical AI Safety Label" certifications, regulatory "sandboxes," and requirements for algorithmic bias testing and explainable AI (XAI). The focus is on establishing rules and guardrails for AI development and deployment. This matters because a lack of trust in the safety and reliability of AI is a major barrier to its adoption.

description: Clarifying Liability and Accountability for AI
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme addresses the critical legal and ethical question of who is responsible when an AI tool makes a mistake that leads to patient harm. It includes calls to clarify liability, considering the roles of developers, end-users (clinicians), and the institutions deploying the technology, and to create "Safe Harbor" protections for clinicians who use certified AI in good faith. This is a distinct governance issue focused on legal responsibility. This matters because unclear liability is a major deterrent for both the development and use of clinical AI.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme addresses the critical legal and ethical question of who is responsible when an AI tool makes a mistake that leads to patient harm. It includes calls to clarify liability, considering the roles of developers, end-users (clinicians), and the institutions deploying the technology, and to create "Safe Harbor" protections for clinicians who use certified AI in good faith. This is a distinct governance issue focused on legal responsibility. This matters because unclear liability is a major deterrent for both the development and use of clinical AI.

description: Patient and Caregiver Empowerment and Rights. This theme focuses on the rights, needs, and experiences of patients and their caregivers within the digital health ecosystem, emphasizing their control over their health and data. It covers issues of data access and control, the right to correct records, the usability of technology, and support for caregivers
detailed_guidelines: This theme focuses on the rights, needs, and experiences of patients and their caregivers within the digital health ecosystem, emphasizing their control over their health and data. It covers issues of data access and control, the right to correct records, the usability of technology, and support for caregivers. This theme is defined by its patient-centric perspective, contrasting with provider- or system-focused themes. It is crucial because many commenters feel the system is designed for institutions, not for the people it is meant to serve.
detailedDescription: This theme focuses on the rights, needs, and experiences of patients and their caregivers within the digital health ecosystem, emphasizing their control over their health and data. It covers issues of data access and control, the right to correct records, the usability of technology, and support for caregivers. This theme is defined by its patient-centric perspective, contrasting with provider- or system-focused themes. It is crucial because many commenters feel the system is designed for institutions, not for the people it is meant to serve.

description: Ensuring Comprehensive Patient Access to Health Data
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme is dedicated to the principle that patients should have complete, easy, and timely access to their health information. This includes calls for a "one-stop shop" to unify fragmented patient portals, the right to access their *entire* record (full EHI) via API beyond just USCDI, and the right to contemporaneous access to finalized test results without provider-imposed delays. This is distinct from provider interoperability, as it focuses on the patient's individual right to their data. This is a fundamental issue for patient advocates who see data access as a matter of safety and autonomy.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme is dedicated to the principle that patients should have complete, easy, and timely access to their health information. This includes calls for a "one-stop shop" to unify fragmented patient portals, the right to access their *entire* record (full EHI) via API beyond just USCDI, and the right to contemporaneous access to finalized test results without provider-imposed delays. This is distinct from provider interoperability, as it focuses on the patient's individual right to their data. This is a fundamental issue for patient advocates who see data access as a matter of safety and autonomy.

description: Patient's Right to Correct and Contribute to Medical Records
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme captures the strong demand for patients to have the ability to correct errors and add context to their own medical records. It includes recommendations to mandate a standardized, FHIR-based process for patients to request corrections and for applications to allow patients to add their own notes or documents, as current processes are described as ineffective. This goes beyond read-only access, advocating for read-write capabilities for patients. This matters because commenters argue that patient records are filled with dangerous errors that can only be fixed by the patient.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme captures the strong demand for patients to have the ability to correct errors and add context to their own medical records. It includes recommendations to mandate a standardized, FHIR-based process for patients to request corrections and for applications to allow patients to add their own notes or documents, as current processes are described as ineffective. This goes beyond read-only access, advocating for read-write capabilities for patients. This matters because commenters argue that patient records are filled with dangerous errors that can only be fixed by the patient.

description: Patient's Right to Data Control and Deletion
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme consolidates comments asserting a patient's fundamental right to control how their data is used and to have it deleted. It includes strong calls for an individual "right to be forgotten" to have health data deleted from systems, and principles that all data collection must be opt-in and based on genuine, informed consent, not buried in long legal documents. This is about patient agency over their data's existence and use. This matters because it reflects a core desire for personal autonomy and control over one's digital footprint.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme consolidates comments asserting a patient's fundamental right to control how their data is used and to have it deleted. It includes strong calls for an individual "right to be forgotten" to have health data deleted from systems, and principles that all data collection must be opt-in and based on genuine, informed consent, not buried in long legal documents. This is about patient agency over their data's existence and use. This matters because it reflects a core desire for personal autonomy and control over one's digital footprint.

description: Improving Usability and Accessibility of Digital Tools
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme addresses the need for digital health tools to be designed for all users, including older adults, those with disabilities, or those with limited digital literacy. It includes recommendations for user-centered design incorporating features like large fonts, voice navigation, multilingual support, and plain language instructions, as well as providing digital literacy training. The defining characteristic is the focus on human-centered design and equitable access. This is important because even the best technology is useless if the intended users cannot operate it.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme addresses the need for digital health tools to be designed for all users, including older adults, those with disabilities, or those with limited digital literacy. It includes recommendations for user-centered design incorporating features like large fonts, voice navigation, multilingual support, and plain language instructions, as well as providing digital literacy training. The defining characteristic is the focus on human-centered design and equitable access. This is important because even the best technology is useless if the intended users cannot operate it.

description: Supporting Family Caregivers with Technology and Access
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme focuses on the unique needs of family caregivers in the digital health ecosystem. It includes recommendations for standardized, secure, and granular proxy access to patient portals and health information, as well as the development and reimbursement of digital tools designed specifically to reduce caregiver burden. This is distinct from general patient access, as it deals with delegated access and the support needs of those caring for others. This matters because caregivers perform essential functions but are often underserved by current technology.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme focuses on the unique needs of family caregivers in the digital health ecosystem. It includes recommendations for standardized, secure, and granular proxy access to patient portals and health information, as well as the development and reimbursement of digital tools designed specifically to reduce caregiver burden. This is distinct from general patient access, as it deals with delegated access and the support needs of those caring for others. This matters because caregivers perform essential functions but are often underserved by current technology.

description: Privacy, Security, and Trust. This theme captures all concerns and recommendations related to protecting health information, establishing trust, and respecting individual liberties in the digital ecosystem. It includes strong opposition to mandatory technologies, concerns about data breaches and surveillance, the need for robust consent management, and calls for new federal privacy laws
detailed_guidelines: This theme captures all concerns and recommendations related to protecting health information, establishing trust, and respecting individual liberties in the digital ecosystem. It includes strong opposition to mandatory technologies, concerns about data breaches and surveillance, the need for robust consent management, and calls for new federal privacy laws. This theme is distinct from interoperability by focusing on the protection and control of data, and often represents a strong counter-narrative to data sharing initiatives. This is a foundational issue of trust for many individual commenters.
detailedDescription: This theme captures all concerns and recommendations related to protecting health information, establishing trust, and respecting individual liberties in the digital ecosystem. It includes strong opposition to mandatory technologies, concerns about data breaches and surveillance, the need for robust consent management, and calls for new federal privacy laws. This theme is distinct from interoperability by focusing on the protection and control of data, and often represents a strong counter-narrative to data sharing initiatives. This is a foundational issue of trust for many individual commenters.

description: Opposition to Mandatory Digital IDs and Technologies
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme captures strong, widespread opposition, primarily from individuals, to any requirement for a digital ID, biometric data, or wearable technology to access healthcare or participate in society. These comments express deep concerns about government control, loss of freedom and autonomy, surveillance, and the potential for such systems to be used to restrict access to services. This is a distinct and highly-charged political and philosophical position. This matters because it represents a significant portion of individual feedback and highlights a deep mistrust of government-led digital initiatives.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme captures strong, widespread opposition, primarily from individuals, to any requirement for a digital ID, biometric data, or wearable technology to access healthcare or participate in society. These comments express deep concerns about government control, loss of freedom and autonomy, surveillance, and the potential for such systems to be used to restrict access to services. This is a distinct and highly-charged political and philosophical position. This matters because it represents a significant portion of individual feedback and highlights a deep mistrust of government-led digital initiatives.

description: Support for Standardized Digital Identity Solutions
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme represents the counter-position, advocating for the adoption of universal, standards-based digital identity credentials to improve security and usability. It includes recommendations to mandate IAL2/AAL2 credentialing, use protocols like OpenID Connect, and leverage federated credentials like Login.gov to reduce patient burden and fraud. The key distinction is the focus on a reusable credential to create a seamless, secure user experience. This is a core recommendation from technology vendors who see it as essential for modernizing healthcare access.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme represents the counter-position, advocating for the adoption of universal, standards-based digital identity credentials to improve security and usability. It includes recommendations to mandate IAL2/AAL2 credentialing, use protocols like OpenID Connect, and leverage federated credentials like Login.gov to reduce patient burden and fraud. The key distinction is the focus on a reusable credential to create a seamless, secure user experience. This is a core recommendation from technology vendors who see it as essential for modernizing healthcare access.

description: Strengthening Data Privacy and Consent Management
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme covers the need for robust patient consent mechanisms and stronger privacy rules. It includes recommendations for API-based granular consent management, clear disclosure of data sharing practices, and a call to pass a comprehensive federal privacy law to protect health data that falls outside of HIPAA. This is about giving patients meaningful control over their privacy choices. This matters because trust in the system depends on patients feeling they have control over their sensitive information, especially as it is used by third-party apps.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme covers the need for robust patient consent mechanisms and stronger privacy rules. It includes recommendations for API-based granular consent management, clear disclosure of data sharing practices, and a call to pass a comprehensive federal privacy law to protect health data that falls outside of HIPAA. This is about giving patients meaningful control over their privacy choices. This matters because trust in the system depends on patients feeling they have control over their sensitive information, especially as it is used by third-party apps.

description: Addressing Systemic Cybersecurity Threats
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme captures comments focused on the threat of major cyberattacks, like ransomware, against healthcare provider organizations. It includes proposals for proactive cybersecurity management, quantitative measurement of hospital cybersecurity posture, and prioritizing operational resilience and patient safety over data privacy during an attack. This is distinct from individual data privacy, focusing on the large-scale, systemic threats to healthcare continuity. This matters because cyberattacks are an increasingly frequent and severe threat to patient safety.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme captures comments focused on the threat of major cyberattacks, like ransomware, against healthcare provider organizations. It includes proposals for proactive cybersecurity management, quantitative measurement of hospital cybersecurity posture, and prioritizing operational resilience and patient safety over data privacy during an attack. This is distinct from individual data privacy, focusing on the large-scale, systemic threats to healthcare continuity. This matters because cyberattacks are an increasingly frequent and severe threat to patient safety.

description: Concerns about Government Data Aggregation and Surveillance
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme captures specific fears about the consolidation of various government and private sector records into a single, accessible database. Commenters express concern that such a system would compromise individual freedom, enable surveillance, and could be misused for political purposes. This is a more specific and politically-focused fear than general data security concerns. This matters as it reflects a deep-seated mistrust in government handling of large, integrated datasets.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme captures specific fears about the consolidation of various government and private sector records into a single, accessible database. Commenters express concern that such a system would compromise individual freedom, enable surveillance, and could be misused for political purposes. This is a more specific and politically-focused fear than general data security concerns. This matters as it reflects a deep-seated mistrust in government handling of large, integrated datasets.

description: Provider and Administrative Burden. This theme encompasses all comments highlighting the administrative, financial, and workflow challenges that technology and regulations impose on healthcare providers and their staff. It includes concerns about documentation and reporting burdens, the high cost of EHR systems, and disruptions to clinical workflows
detailed_guidelines: This theme encompasses all comments highlighting the administrative, financial, and workflow challenges that technology and regulations impose on healthcare providers and their staff. It includes concerns about documentation and reporting burdens, the high cost of EHR systems, and disruptions to clinical workflows. This theme is defined by its focus on the day-to-day operational pain points for providers. This is a critical issue because provider burnout and administrative overload are seen as major barriers to improving care.
detailedDescription: This theme encompasses all comments highlighting the administrative, financial, and workflow challenges that technology and regulations impose on healthcare providers and their staff. It includes concerns about documentation and reporting burdens, the high cost of EHR systems, and disruptions to clinical workflows. This theme is defined by its focus on the day-to-day operational pain points for providers. This is a critical issue because provider burnout and administrative overload are seen as major barriers to improving care.

description: Reducing Documentation and Reporting Burdens
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme focuses on specific recommendations to alleviate the workload of clinical documentation and quality reporting. It includes calls to automate quality reporting via Bulk FHIR with centralized computation, streamline compliance requirements, digitize FMLA/ADA forms, reduce the OASIS assessment for home health, and use AI to auto-populate clinical notes. This is distinct from general cost concerns, focusing on time and effort spent on paperwork. This is a top-of-mind issue for providers who feel they spend more time on documentation than with patients.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme focuses on specific recommendations to alleviate the workload of clinical documentation and quality reporting. It includes calls to automate quality reporting via Bulk FHIR with centralized computation, streamline compliance requirements, digitize FMLA/ADA forms, reduce the OASIS assessment for home health, and use AI to auto-populate clinical notes. This is distinct from general cost concerns, focusing on time and effort spent on paperwork. This is a top-of-mind issue for providers who feel they spend more time on documentation than with patients.

description: Addressing EHR and Health IT Costs and Complexity
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme covers the financial and operational challenges associated with acquiring, integrating, and maintaining health IT. It includes concerns from small, rural, and safety-net practices about the exorbitant cost of EHR systems, high fees for data access from vendors, and the complexity of meeting certification requirements. It also includes calls for targeted financial and technical assistance, such as a revived Health IT Extension Program. This is distinct from documentation burden, focusing on the technology's cost and difficulty. This matters because these are significant barriers to adoption for smaller providers.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme covers the financial and operational challenges associated with acquiring, integrating, and maintaining health IT. It includes concerns from small, rural, and safety-net practices about the exorbitant cost of EHR systems, high fees for data access from vendors, and the complexity of meeting certification requirements. It also includes calls for targeted financial and technical assistance, such as a revived Health IT Extension Program. This is distinct from documentation burden, focusing on the technology's cost and difficulty. This matters because these are significant barriers to adoption for smaller providers.

description: Aligning "Incident to" Billing with Data Reality
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme details the specific concern that "incident to" billing practices, where services rendered by a PA or other professional are billed under a physician's NPI, distort data. This practice makes it impossible to accurately assess the quality, cost, and contribution of care provided by non-physician clinicians. The recommendation is to reform this practice to ensure accurate data attribution for quality measurement and workforce evaluation. This matters for improving data accuracy, transparency, and fair evaluation of all clinician types.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme details the specific concern that "incident to" billing practices, where services rendered by a PA or other professional are billed under a physician's NPI, distort data. This practice makes it impossible to accurately assess the quality, cost, and contribution of care provided by non-physician clinicians. The recommendation is to reform this practice to ensure accurate data attribution for quality measurement and workforce evaluation. This matters for improving data accuracy, transparency, and fair evaluation of all clinician types.

description: Managing In-Basket and Patient Portal Message Burden
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme focuses on the specific and significant burden of managing the electronic in-basket and patient portal messages in EHRs. It includes recommendations for EHRs to better track the time physicians spend on message management and for CMS to consider new payment models or codes to compensate clinicians for this growing volume of uncompensated work. This is a major driver of physician burnout. This matters because the explosion in electronic messages has created a significant strain on the clinical workforce.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme focuses on the specific and significant burden of managing the electronic in-basket and patient portal messages in EHRs. It includes recommendations for EHRs to better track the time physicians spend on message management and for CMS to consider new payment models or codes to compensate clinicians for this growing volume of uncompensated work. This is a major driver of physician burnout. This matters because the explosion in electronic messages has created a significant strain on the clinical workforce.

description: Value-Based Care (VBC) and Payment Model Reform. This theme groups all comments related to the design, implementation, and success of value-based care models, and how technology and payment policy can support them. It covers the data infrastructure needed for VBC, the integration of digital health into payment models, the alignment of quality measures, and the need for new reimbursement models
detailed_guidelines: This theme groups all comments related to the design, implementation, and success of value-based care models, and how technology and payment policy can support them. It covers the data infrastructure needed for VBC, the integration of digital health into payment models, the alignment of quality measures, and the need for new reimbursement models. This theme is about aligning financial incentives with the goals of high-value, technology-enabled care. This is critical as many commenters see VBC's success as dependent on a modern data infrastructure.
detailedDescription: This theme groups all comments related to the design, implementation, and success of value-based care models, and how technology and payment policy can support them. It covers the data infrastructure needed for VBC, the integration of digital health into payment models, the alignment of quality measures, and the need for new reimbursement models. This theme is about aligning financial incentives with the goals of high-value, technology-enabled care. This is critical as many commenters see VBC's success as dependent on a modern data infrastructure.

description: Meeting the Critical Data Needs of VBC
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme focuses on the data infrastructure required for VBC models to succeed. It includes demands for timely access to performance data (claims, quality, financial), real-time admission, discharge, transfer (ADT) feeds, and standardized, two-way data exchange between payers and providers. It also includes concerns that VBC entities are "flying blind" due to data delays and fragmentation. This is distinct from general interoperability by tying data access directly to the operational needs of managing risk. This is a core concern for providers in VBC contracts.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme focuses on the data infrastructure required for VBC models to succeed. It includes demands for timely access to performance data (claims, quality, financial), real-time admission, discharge, transfer (ADT) feeds, and standardized, two-way data exchange between payers and providers. It also includes concerns that VBC entities are "flying blind" due to data delays and fragmentation. This is distinct from general interoperability by tying data access directly to the operational needs of managing risk. This is a core concern for providers in VBC contracts.

description: Integrating and Incentivizing Digital Health in VBC
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme covers recommendations to better incorporate and pay for digital health tools within APMs. It includes proposals to provide incentives (e.g., infrastructure stipends, shared savings bonuses) for ACOs to adopt digital tools, include digital therapeutics uptake in contracts, and create a "VBC-Ready EMR Certification." The key focus is on creating a business case for technology within VBC. This is important for driving the adoption of innovative tools that can help achieve VBC goals.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme covers recommendations to better incorporate and pay for digital health tools within APMs. It includes proposals to provide incentives (e.g., infrastructure stipends, shared savings bonuses) for ACOs to adopt digital tools, include digital therapeutics uptake in contracts, and create a "VBC-Ready EMR Certification." The key focus is on creating a business case for technology within VBC. This is important for driving the adoption of innovative tools that can help achieve VBC goals.

description: Modernizing Quality Measurement for VBC
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme addresses issues with how quality is measured and reported in VBC models. This includes calls to standardize quality measures across payers, transition to automated digital quality measures (dQMs) submitted via Bulk FHIR, reduce the reporting burden, and include patient refusal as a valid exclusion in measure logic. This is distinct from data access, focusing on the metrics themselves and the reporting process. This is a major pain point for providers overwhelmed by fragmented quality reporting requirements.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme addresses issues with how quality is measured and reported in VBC models. This includes calls to standardize quality measures across payers, transition to automated digital quality measures (dQMs) submitted via Bulk FHIR, reduce the reporting burden, and include patient refusal as a valid exclusion in measure logic. This is distinct from data access, focusing on the metrics themselves and the reporting process. This is a major pain point for providers overwhelmed by fragmented quality reporting requirements.

description: Establishing Clear Reimbursement for Innovative Technology
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme focuses on the need for predictable and adequate payment for new digital health technologies that don't fit existing reimbursement categories. It includes calls for structured payment pathways for new technologies (e.g., AI, interventional software), an outpatient equivalent to the inpatient NTAP program, and new reimbursement models like "Fee-for-Knowledge-Sharing." This is distinct from VBC incentives, focusing on fostering innovation by reducing financial uncertainty for new technologies. This is a key issue for technology developers.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme focuses on the need for predictable and adequate payment for new digital health technologies that don't fit existing reimbursement categories. It includes calls for structured payment pathways for new technologies (e.g., AI, interventional software), an outpatient equivalent to the inpatient NTAP program, and new reimbursement models like "Fee-for-Knowledge-Sharing." This is distinct from VBC incentives, focusing on fostering innovation by reducing financial uncertainty for new technologies. This is a key issue for technology developers.

description: Health Equity and the Digital Divide. This theme clusters all comments that address the impact of digital health on health equity and the specific needs of vulnerable or underserved populations. This includes concerns about the digital divide (access to broadband, devices, and skills), and the needs of rural communities, older adults, safety-net providers, and other specific populations
detailed_guidelines: This theme clusters all comments that address the impact of digital health on health equity and the specific needs of vulnerable or underserved populations. This includes concerns about the digital divide (access to broadband, devices, and skills), and the needs of rural communities, older adults, safety-net providers, and other specific populations. This theme is defined by its focus on ensuring that the digital transformation of healthcare does not leave certain groups behind. This is a cross-cutting issue of high importance to policymakers and advocacy groups.
detailedDescription: This theme clusters all comments that address the impact of digital health on health equity and the specific needs of vulnerable or underserved populations. This includes concerns about the digital divide (access to broadband, devices, and skills), and the needs of rural communities, older adults, safety-net providers, and other specific populations. This theme is defined by its focus on ensuring that the digital transformation of healthcare does not leave certain groups behind. This is a cross-cutting issue of high importance to policymakers and advocacy groups.

description: Addressing the Digital Divide
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme focuses on barriers to digital health adoption related to technology access and skills. It includes concerns about the lack of reliable internet/broadband coverage, patients' lack of access to devices, and issues with digital literacy, particularly among older adults, lower-income individuals, and rural populations. Recommendations call for federal support for broadband expansion and digital navigator programs. The focus is on the foundational infrastructure and skills needed to participate in digital health. This is a critical prerequisite for the success of nearly all proposed digital health initiatives.
detailedDescription: This sub-theme focuses on barriers to digital health adoption related to technology access and skills. It includes concerns about the lack of reliable internet/broadband coverage, patients' lack of access to devices, and issues with digital literacy, particularly among older adults, lower-income individuals, and rural populations. Recommendations call for federal support for broadband expansion and digital navigator programs. The focus is on the foundational infrastructure and skills needed to participate in digital health. This is a critical prerequisite for the success of nearly all proposed digital health initiatives.

description: Supporting Small, Rural, and Safety-Net Providers
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme addresses the unique challenges faced by small, rural, under-resourced, and post-acute care providers. It includes concerns about their inability to afford expensive IT systems, lack of dedicated staff, and historical exclusion from incentive programs. Recommendations call for targeted funding, regulatory waivers, and technical assistance (like a revived Health IT Extension Program). This is distinct from the patient-focused digital divide, concentrating on provider-side resource constraints. This matters because these practices are often the sole source of care in their communities.

description: Integrating Social Determinants of Health (SDOH) Data
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme covers the need to standardize, collect, and meaningfully use data about non-clinical factors that impact health outcomes. It includes recommendations to enhance USCDI to include structured SDOH elements, integrate social risk analytics into VBC models, and support e-referrals to community-based organizations to address identified needs. The focus is on integrating non-clinical factors like housing, transportation, and food security into the healthcare ecosystem. This matters because addressing SDOH is seen as crucial for achieving health equity.

description: Ensuring Equitable and Inclusive Technology Design
detailed_guidelines: This sub-theme focuses on the need to design and evaluate technology with equity as a primary goal. It includes recommendations to prioritize input from diverse beneficiaries in technology development, ensure tools are culturally appropriate and available in multiple languages, and include equity metrics in evaluation frameworks (e.g., uptake rates across racial and geographic groups). This is about building equity into the technology itself. This matters because poorly designed tools can perpetuate or worsen existing disparities.
